source.title: Quantization stability & SLO tradeoffs - KV warmup, batching interaction, on-prem considerations
next_steps: Redwood to run 24h warmup + KV-staleness benchmark and share results (Ava)
Nimbus to provide 3 representative prompt sets and SLA targets (Priya)
Exchange security checklist and KMS integration docs (Samir)
Follow-up call to review benchmark + regression signals (Ava & Priya)
action_items: Ava Chen (Redwood SE): run end-to-end warmup test with kv-populated replay, attach perf logs (due 2026-10-14)
Priya Rao (Nimbus): share 3 production prompt sets + golden outputs for accuracy checks (due 2026-10-12)
Samir Patel (Redwood AE): send on-prem network topology checklist and pricing ballpark (due 2026-10-09)
Marco Diaz (Nimbus SRE): provision test nodes and open ports for remote benchmarking (due 2026-10-13)
source.transcript:
Meeting header: 2026-10-07 15:30 UTC | Duration: ~72 min | Attendees: Samir (Redwood AE), Ava (Redwood SE), Priya (Nimbus ML Infra), Marco (Nimbus SRE), Helen (Nimbus Eng Lead)

Summary (auto): technical deep dive on quantization-induced stability and SLO impact. Discussed KV cache warmup strategies, batching interactions that amplify apparent accuracy regressions after INT8/AWQ conversion, on-prem networking considerations, and a plan for a warmup + regression benchmark. Agreed to exchange prompt sets and run a 24-hour warmup test.

00:00 Samir (Redwood AE): Alright everyone, thanks for joining — we'll run about an hourish deep dive on the quantization artifacts you saw, focusing on KV cache warmup, batching, and how that shows up in on-prem setups. Ava will lead the bench details.
00:22 Ava (Redwood SE): Hi hi, thanks. Quick ask before we start: Priya, can you confirm the model variant you're running? You mentioned a 7B variant but I want the exact— is it the community gpt-7b-xl or the custom-tuned fork?
00:36 Priya (Nimbus): It's the gpt-seven-b fork with a tiny finetune for medical parsing. Sorry my shorthand, it's the -med-tune variant.
00:44 Ava: Cool, that matters because kernel selection and quant mapping can differ a bit. Also are you on TF32/FP16 baseline or pure FP32 currently?
00:56 Priya: Baseline was FP16 on our on-prem A100s, we tried INT8 via gptq and then AWQ as an alternative. The first thing we noticed was sporadic hallucination spikes on longer prompts.
01:14 Samir: Got it. Could you describe the workload pattern — short chatty requests vs long document decoding?
01:22 Marco (Nimbus SRE): Mostly multi-turn, often warm-started from stored prefixes, so KV cache reuse is common. Peak QPS is moderate — 45-60 reqs/s — but tail latency is critical: 95th under 200ms.
01:38 Ava: That tail SLO is helpful. We often see quantization regressions amplified at tail due to batching and stale KV cache interactions. I'll sketch that out now.
02:05 Ava: (screen share ref) Simplified flow: request arrives -> router picks GPU -> runtime may batch -> KV cache hit/miss -> decode. If quant change shifts logits slightly, early tokens can cause cache divergence — leading to different cached states and compounded error.
02:35 Priya: Sorry, explain "cache divergence" again? We thought KV cache was just bytes saved, why would quantization change that?
02:44 Ava: Totally. So KV cache stores per-step keys/values. If the quantized model produces different hidden activations even by small epsilon, the keys/values differ. Downstream, when those cached tokens are reused as prefix, the newer values are not bit-identical, so their contribution changes, and errors can accumulate over a long decode.
03:05 Priya: Okay that makes sense, small numeric shift cascades.
03:12 Samir: Also worth noting kernel ordering and rounding differences across A100 drivers — sometimes the same quant file behaves slightly differently across driver versions.
03:26 Marco: We had one node that showed better accuracy than others; I assumed hardware flakiness. Could driver mismatch be the cause?
03:34 Ava: Absolutely. We've seen mismatched cuBLAS/cuDNN/kernel selection cause tiny differences. We add a checksum step in our deploy to detect cross-node differences.
03:50 Helen (Nimbus): On batching — we use dynamic batching to keep latency down. Is there any rule-of-thumb how batch size interacts with INT8/AWQ?
04:03 Ava: Two effects: first, effective sequence alignment inside batch can cause different per-step compute paths. Second, batching increases the chance a request will read a KV entry written under a different numeric regime if you're mixing model variants. For pure single-variant deploys, larger batches mainly affect latency, not accuracy, but they can amplify timing jitter which sometimes looks like accuracy noise.
04:30 Priya: When you say "different numeric regime" do you mean we might mix fp16 and int8 in routing?
04:37 Samir: Yes, in hybrid setups we route to fallbacks or canaries that might be quantized differently. That mixed traffic is a big vector for apparent regression.
04:48 Marco: We do have canary routing during rollout, but we didn't realize it could cause cache contamination.
04:55 Ava: Exactly — if a canary writes keys to a shared KV layer or if sticky routing isn't strict, subsequent requests hitting another variant will see the stale keys. In Redwood private we isolate KV per variant or use per-instance caches to avoid this.
05:20 Priya: Our on-prem config is shared NVMe-backed cache to save memory, so it's shared across pods. Hmm.
05:29 Ava: That explains a lot. Shared persistent caches are higher risk unless you version the cache key namespace with model id+quant tag.
05:40 Samir: Quick aside on warmup: we recommend a multi-phase warmup. Short 1-2 hour synthetic warmup is often insufficient. The patterns you care about — long multi-turn prefixes — take longer to populate and surface staleness.
06:00 Priya: We did a 2 hour warmup and thought we were fine. You're suggesting 24 hours?
06:07 Ava: At scale, yes. Especially if you have a low-frequency long-tail prompt. We propose: 1) synthetic warmup to exercise kernels; 2) replay of production prefixes for 24h to populate the KV. That surface regressions that only appear after repeated reuse.
06:26 Marco: Sound doable. Does that require holding GPU memory for the KV longer? We worry about memory pressure for multi-tenant nodes.
06:34 Ava: You can use sticky cache populations: replay to each node and then GC older entries; or use a compact KV with truncation policy tuned to your prefix length distribution. Redwood's runtime supports max-kv-entries per model instance and LRU/TTL policies.
06:52 Priya: About AWQ vs GPTQ — we tried AWQ after GPTQ looked noisy. AWQ reduced some hallucinations but introduced a different slowness pattern. Is that expected?
07:05 Ava: Yes. AWQ uses asymmetric quantization which can preserve ranges better but may change rounding behavior — that can alter softmax dynamics, which can improve certain probabilities but also shift token ordering in edge cases. GPTQ sometimes underestimates outlier weights and can bias toward safer tokens but lose nuance. There's no one-size-fits-all.
07:35 Samir: Also inference kernel choices differ — AWQ gets a different optimized kernel path that may have slightly higher latency per token. We can show kernel-level traces.
07:48 Marco: If AWQ adds latency, that could push your 95th over 200ms in our traffic. Any mitigations?
07:57 Ava: Several: 1) micro-batching (we use continuous batching with dynamic max batch size), 2) prefix caching to avoid recomputing earlier layers, 3) model pipelining or using faster kernel for early layers and AWQ for later layers — hybrid precision. But hybrid introduces complexity in correctness.
08:25 Priya: Hybrid precision — do you mean fp16 for first N layers, int8 for last layers?
08:33 Ava: Correct. Often earlier layers contain more signal, later layers can be quantized more aggressively. But you have to maintain KV key format compatibility. If your keys are created by mixed-precision runs, reuse must be consistent.
08:49 Priya: That's scary for us. We're constrained to deterministic outputs for regulatory reasons in some workflows.
08:56 Samir: For regulatory paths, we suggest canarying with deterministic checksums and automated rollback. Also document the acceptable delta thresholds for token distributions.
09:10 Helen: On deterministic checksums — do you calc over logits or final tokens?
09:15 Ava: Both. We do a per-token logit-delta histogram and a final-token-match rate. For your use case, final-token-match plus key-checksums for KV should be required.
09:27 Marco: You mentioned earlier that driver versions can shift kernels. How strict should our infra be?
09:33 Ava: Pin GPU driver, CUDA, and runtime libs across nodes for production. We recommend a canary pool with the exact same stack and periodic checksum runs across nodes to catch drift.
09:45 Priya: Question on batching: we observed that when we moved from batch size 1 to 4, some prompts suddenly regressed. Should we just keep batch size 1?
09:55 Ava: Not necessarily. Try to align sequence lengths inside batch (bucketing) to reduce padding-induced artifacts and use per-request sequence-aware caching. Also enable per-request latency caps — Redwood supports tail-targeted batching where requests close to SLO avoid joining big batches.
10:18 Samir: Another gotcha: random seed for sampling. If your eval uses nucleus sampling with temperature, small logit shifts change sample results dramatically. For deterministic evals, use greedy decode or fixed seeds where possible.
10:35 Priya: Our golden set uses stochastic decodes for nuance, so we can't just switch. We need an eval that mirrors production sampling.
10:44 Ava: Then compute distributional metrics (KL divergence, ngram overlap) and track changes rather than per-example token equality. That reduces noise sensitivity.
10:56 Marco: On on-prem networking — we expect 2ms ± jitter inside cluster, but occasionally nodes hit 10-20ms for NVMe reads. Does that matter for KV?
11:06 Ava: It does. If your KV is NVMe-backed and shared over network, latency spikes create asynchronous staleness windows. Those windows combined with batching can lead to a request reading slightly older keys than expected. For SLOs under 200ms, aim for sub-5ms stable KV access or local in-memory caches per instance with periodic persistence.
11:30 Priya: That's useful. Any suggestions for persistent cache namespace versioning?
11:36 Ava: Prefix cache keys with model-id:quant-tag:kernel-hash:deployment-id. That way, mismatched kernels or quant tags never reuse another variant's keys.
11:50 Priya: Good. We'll need that change.
11:54 Samir: Quick break — we'll pivot to the proposed benchmark plan. Ava?
12:00 Ava: Plan: 1) Baseline FP16 steady-state run with your production prompts to capture golden outputs. 2) Apply GPTQ and AWQ variants, run an isolated 24h replay of real prefixes across nodes to populate KV. 3) During replay, measure per-token logit deltas, final token divergence, p95/p99 latency, and kernel-level timings. 4) Run a staggered warmdown to see if regressions persist after GC.
12:34 Marco: For the 24h replay, can we limit to a subset of prefixes to reduce load?
12:39 Ava: Yes, pick the heavy-weight long-tail prefixes and high-risk prompt families. The goal is to replicate reuse patterns. Priya, that's where your example prompt sets come in.
12:51 Priya: We'll assemble three buckets: short chatty, long-form multi-turn medical case, and adversarial edge cases. We'll include golden outputs for each.
13:03 Samir: On measurement: we'll provide a small script to compute per-step KL and token-match, plus a dashboard with kernel traces. We'll also surface a simple signal: consecutive-run regression count (how many examples show worse metric vs baseline).
13:19 Helen: About staging: we prefer not to hit production GPUs for this. Should we spin a mirror cluster?
13:26 Ava: For best fidelity, mirror cluster with same driver stack is ideal. If cost is a constraint, we can run sequential replays on each node in smaller batches but that lengthens wall time.
13:38 Marco: We'll try to provision a 4-node mirror.
13:42 Priya: Any fast wins to try before the full run?
13:46 Ava: Yes: 1) Namespace the KV to prevent cross-variant contamination. 2) Pin drivers. 3) Run a 6h focused replay on the long-form bucket and inspect KL deltas — often that surfaces the major issues.
14:03 Samir: Let's capture the potential root cause matrix: numeric precision changes, kernel selection, KV cross-variant contamination, batching alignment, NVMe network jitter, and sampling stochasticity. We'll rank them by probability once we see the trace.
14:18 Priya: One more question: do you have an existing tool for per-token logit histograms? Or do we need to build?
14:25 Ava: Redwood has an eval agent that can instrument per-layer logits and produce histograms; we'll provide a minimal config to run it and a small collector script. We'll attach to the follow-up.
14:36 Marco: Security question — for on-prem pilot, how will Redwood access our cluster? We prefer SSH jump with restricted keys, no open inbound.
14:44 Samir: We support agent-based connectors or a temporary bastion. For sensitive clusters we can do air-gapped data exchange: you run the benchmark and upload the logs to a secure bucket for us to analyze. We'll share the security checklist after this call.
14:59 Helen: KMS integration — we use in-house HSM. Is that supported for model encryption or just key wrapping for artifacts?
15:06 Ava: We support KMS/HSM integration for key wrapping of model artifacts and for traffic to private control plane in Redwood Private. For on-prem, we can integrate via standard PKCS11/KMS adapters. We'll add docs.
15:20 Priya: Great.
15:25 Samir: Okay we are nearing the end. Quick recap: Redwood will run the proposed warmup + 24h replay on a mirror cluster (Ava), Nimbus will share prompt sets and provision mirror nodes (Priya/Marco), Samir will send the security and topology checklist and price ballpark. We'll reconvene with benchmark results mid-next week.
15:45 Helen: One tiny nit — when you run the bench, can you also include a run with pinned seed greedy decode so we can separate sampling noise from systematic drift?
15:53 Ava: Yep, we'll include greedy and stochastic modes.
15:57 Marco: And can you log kernel version/hash with each result?
16:01 Ava: Yes, kernel and driver checksum will be attached.
16:05 Priya: Thanks. That was useful.
16:09 Samir: Thanks everyone. We'll follow up with the artifacts.
16:12 (end)

Action items and artifacts referenced: 
- Redwood benchmark plan doc (link): https://redwood-internal/example-benchmarks/kv-warmup-plan (redwood shared link)
- Security checklist (will be sent): includes KMS adapters, PKCS11 notes, required ports and bastion setup
- Eval scripts: redwood-eval-agent v2.3 (attached in CRM record)

Notes on transcription: few speaker label swaps at 03:00-03:20 where automated system labeled Samir as Speaker 1; some technical tokens (AWQ/GPTQ/drivers) mis-transcribed in spots as AQW/GPP-TQ.

01:22 Marco (Nimbus SRE): Mostly multi-turn, often warm-started from stored prefixes, so KV cache reuse is common. Peak QPS is moderate — 45-60 reqs/s — but tail latency is critical: 95th under 200ms.
01:38 Ava: That tail SLO is helpful. We often see quantization regressions amplified at tail due to batching and stale KV cache interactions. I'll sketch that out now.
02:05 Ava: (screen share ref) Simplified flow: request arrives -> router picks GPU -> runtime may batch -> KV cache hit/miss -> decode. If quant change shifts logits slightly, early tokens can cause cache divergence — leading to different cached states and compounded error.
02:35 Priya: Sorry, explain \"cache divergence\" again? We thought KV cache was just bytes saved, why would quantization change that?
02:44 Ava: Totally. So KV cache stores per-step keys/values. If the quantized model produces different hidden activations even by small epsilon, the keys/values differ. Downstream, when those cached tokens are reused as prefix, the newer values are not bit-identical, so their contribution changes, and errors can accumulate over a long decode.
03:05 Priya: Okay that makes sense, small numeric shift cascades.
03:12 Samir: Also worth noting kernel ordering and rounding differences across A100 drivers — sometimes the same quant file behaves slightly differently across driver versions.
03:26 Marco: We had one node that showed better accuracy than others; I assumed hardware flakiness. Could driver mismatch be the cause?
03:34 Ava: Absolutely. We've seen mismatched cuBLAS/cuDNN/kernel selection cause tiny differences. We add a checksum step in our deploy to detect cross-node differences.
03:50 Helen (Nimbus): On batching — we use dynamic batching to keep latency down. Is there any rule-of-thumb how batch size interacts with INT8/AWQ?
04:03 Ava: Two effects: first, effective sequence alignment inside batch can cause different per-step compute paths. Second, batching increases the chance a request will read a KV entry written under a different numeric regime if you're mixing model variants. For pure single-variant deploys, larger batches mainly affect latency, not accuracy, but they can amplify timing jitter which sometimes looks like accuracy noise.
04:30 Priya: When you say \"different numeric regime\" do you mean we might mix fp16 and int8 in routing?
04:37 Samir: Yes, in hybrid setups we route to fallbacks or canaries that might be quantized differently. That mixed traffic is a big vector for apparent regression.
04:48 Marco: We do have canary routing during rollout, but we didn't realize it could cause cache contamination.
04:55 Ava: Exactly — if a canary writes keys to a shared KV layer or if sticky routing isn't strict, subsequent requests hitting another variant will see the stale keys. In Redwood private we isolate KV per variant or use per-instance caches to avoid this.
05:20 Priya: Our on-prem config is shared NVMe-backed cache to save memory, so it's shared across pods. Hmm.
05:29 Ava: That explains a lot. Shared persistent caches are higher risk unless you version the cache key namespace with model id+quant tag.
05:40 Samir: Quick aside on warmup: we recommend a multi-phase warmup. Short 1-2 hour synthetic warmup is often insufficient. The patterns you care about — long multi-turn prefixes — take longer to populate and surface staleness.
06:00 Priya: We did a 2 hour warmup and thought we were fine. You're suggesting 24 hours?
06:07 Ava: At scale, yes. Especially if you have a low-frequency long-tail prompt. We propose: 1) synthetic warmup to exercise kernels; 2) replay of production prefixes for 24h to populate the KV. That surface regressions that only appear after repeated reuse.
06:26 Marco: Sound doable. Does that require holding GPU memory for the KV longer? We worry about memory pressure for multi-tenant nodes.
06:34 Ava: You can use sticky cache populations: replay to each node and then GC older entries; or use a compact KV with truncation policy tuned to your prefix length distribution. Redwood's runtime supports max-kv-entries per model instance and LRU/TTL policies.
06:52 Priya: About AWQ vs GPTQ — we tried AWQ after GPTQ looked noisy. AWQ reduced some hallucinations but introduced a different slowness pattern. Is that expected?
07:05 Ava: Yes. AWQ uses asymmetric quantization which can preserve ranges better but may change rounding behavior — that can alter softmax dynamics, which can improve certain probabilities but also shift token ordering in edge cases. GPTQ sometimes underestimates outlier weights and can bias toward safer tokens but lose nuance. There's no one-size-fits-all.
07:35 Samir: Also inference kernel choices differ — AWQ gets a different optimized kernel path that may have slightly higher latency per token. We can show kernel-level traces.
07:48 Marco: If AWQ adds latency, that could push your 95th over 200ms in our traffic. Any mitigations?
07:57 Ava: Several: 1) micro-batching (we use continuous batching with dynamic max batch size), 2) prefix caching to avoid recomputing earlier layers, 3) model pipelining or using faster kernel for early layers and AWQ for later layers — hybrid precision. But hybrid introduces complexity in correctness.
08:25 Priya: Hybrid precision — do you mean fp16 for first N layers, int8 for last layers?
08:33 Ava: Correct. Often earlier layers contain more signal, later layers can be quantized more aggressively. But you have to maintain KV key format compatibility. If your keys are created by mixed-precision runs, reuse must be consistent.
08:49 Priya: That's scary for us. We're constrained to deterministic outputs for regulatory reasons in some workflows.
08:56 Samir: For regulatory paths, we suggest canarying with deterministic checksums and automated rollback. Also document the acceptable delta thresholds for token distributions.
09:10 Helen: On deterministic checksums — do you calc over logits or final tokens?
09:15 Ava: Both. We do a per-token logit-delta histogram and a final-token-match rate. For your use case, final-token-match plus key-checksums for KV should be required.
09:27 Marco: You mentioned earlier that driver versions can shift kernels. How strict should our infra be?
09:33 Ava: Pin GPU driver, CUDA, and runtime libs across nodes for production. We recommend a canary pool with the exact same stack and periodic checksum runs across nodes to catch drift.
09:45 Priya: Question on batching: we observed that when we moved from batch size 1 to 4, some prompts suddenly regressed. Should we just keep batch size 1?
09:55 Ava: Not necessarily. Try to align sequence lengths inside batch (bucketing) to reduce padding-induced artifacts and use per-request sequence-aware caching. Also enable per-request latency caps — Redwood supports tail-targeted batching where requests close to SLO avoid joining big batches.
10:18 Samir: Another gotcha: random seed for sampling. If your eval uses nucleus sampling with temperature, small logit shifts change sample results dramatically. For deterministic evals, use greedy decode or fixed seeds where possible.
10:35 Priya: Our golden set uses stochastic decodes for nuance, so we can't just switch. We need an eval that mirrors production sampling.
10:44 Ava: Then compute distributional metrics (KL divergence, ngram overlap) and track changes rather than per-example token equality. That reduces noise sensitivity.
10:56 Marco: On on-prem networking — we expect 2ms ± jitter inside cluster, but occasionally nodes hit 10-20ms for NVMe reads. Does that matter for KV?
11:06 Ava: It does. If your KV is NVMe-backed and shared over network, latency spikes create asynchronous staleness windows. Those windows combined with batching can lead to a request reading slightly older keys than expected. For SLOs under 200ms, aim for sub-5ms stable KV access or local in-memory caches per instance with periodic persistence.
11:30 Priya: That's useful. Any suggestions for persistent cache namespace versioning?
11:36 Ava: Prefix cache keys with model-id:quant-tag:kernel-hash:deployment-id. That way, mismatched kernels or quant tags never reuse another variant's keys.
11:50 Priya: Good. We'll need that change.
11:54 Samir: Quick break — we'll pivot to the proposed benchmark plan. Ava?
12:00 Ava: Plan: 1) Baseline FP16 steady-state run with your production prompts to capture golden outputs. 2) Apply GPTQ and AWQ variants, run an isolated 24h replay of real prefixes across nodes to populate KV. 3) During replay, measure per-token logit deltas, final token divergence, p95/p99 latency, and kernel-level timings. 4) Run a staggered warmdown to see if regressions persist after GC.
12:34 Marco: For the 24h replay, can we limit to a subset of prefixes to reduce load?
12:39 Ava: Yes, pick the heavy-weight long-tail prefixes and high-risk prompt families. The goal is to replicate reuse patterns. Priya, that's where your example prompt sets come in.
12:51 Priya: We'll assemble three buckets: short chatty, long-form multi-turn medical case, and adversarial edge cases. We'll include golden outputs for each.
13:03 Samir: On measurement: we'll provide a small script to compute per-step KL and token-match, plus a dashboard with kernel traces. We'll also surface a simple signal: consecutive-run regression count (how many examples show worse metric vs baseline).
13:19 Helen: About staging: we prefer not to hit production GPUs for this. Should we spin a mirror cluster?
13:26 Ava: For best fidelity, mirror cluster with same driver stack is ideal. If cost is a constraint, we can run sequential replays on each node in smaller batches but that lengthens wall time.
13:38 Marco: We'll try to provision a 4-node mirror.
13:42 Priya: Any fast wins to try before the full run?
13:46 Ava: Yes: 1) Namespace the KV to prevent cross-variant contamination. 2) Pin drivers. 3) Run a 6h focused replay on the long-form bucket and inspect KL deltas — often that surfaces the major issues.
14:03 Samir: Let's capture the potential root cause matrix: numeric precision changes, kernel selection, KV cross-variant contamination, batching alignment, NVMe network jitter, and sampling stochasticity. We'll rank them by probability once we see the trace.
14:18 Priya: One more question: do you have an existing tool for per-token logit histograms? Or do we need to build?
14:25 Ava: Redwood has an eval agent that can instrument per-layer logits and produce histograms; we'll provide a minimal config to run it and a small collector script. We'll attach to the follow-up.
14:36 Marco: Security question — for on-prem pilot, how will Redwood access our cluster? We prefer SSH jump with restricted keys, no open inbound.
14:44 Samir: We support agent-based connectors or a temporary bastion. For sensitive clusters we can do air-gapped data exchange: you run the benchmark and upload the logs to a secure bucket for us to analyze. We'll share the security checklist after this call.
14:59 Helen: KMS integration — we use in-house HSM. Is that supported for model encryption or just key wrapping for artifacts?
15:06 Ava: We support KMS/HSM integration for key wrapping of model artifacts and for traffic to private control plane in Redwood Private. For on-prem, we can integrate via standard PKCS11/KMS adapters. We'll add docs.
15:20 Priya: Great.
15:25 Samir: Okay we are nearing the end. Quick recap: Redwood will run the proposed warmup + 24h replay on a mirror cluster (Ava), Nimbus will share prompt sets and provision mirror nodes (Priya/Marco), Samir will send the security and topology checklist and price ballpark. We'll reconvene with benchmark results mid-next week.
15:45 Helen: One tiny nit — when you run the bench, can you also include a run with pinned seed greedy decode so we can separate sampling noise from systematic drift?
15:53 Ava: Yep, we'll include greedy and stochastic modes.
15:57 Marco: And can you log kernel version/hash with each result?
16:01 Ava: Yes, kernel and driver checksum will be attached.
16:05 Priya: Thanks. That was useful.
16:09 Samir: Thanks everyone. We'll follow up with the artifacts.
16:12 (end)

Action items and artifacts referenced: 
- Redwood benchmark plan doc (link): https://redwood-internal/example-benchmarks/kv-warmup-plan (redwood shared link)
- Security checklist (will be sent): includes KMS adapters, PKCS11 notes, required ports and bastion setup
- Eval scripts: redwood-eval-agent v2.3 (attached in CRM record)

Notes on transcription: few speaker label swaps at 03:00-03:20 where automated system labeled Samir as Speaker 1; some technical tokens (AWQ/GPTQ/drivers) mis-transcribed in spots as AQW/GPP-TQ.

source.title: Security review: EU/US region pinning + KMS routing - Nimbus Analytics
next_steps: Sofia to send current subprocessor inventory + DPA excerpt
Ethan to run a short perf benchmark for EU-pin vs US-fallback
Nimbus to provide sample data residency policy and target SLA requirements
Schedule follow-up call for KMS onboarding demo week of 2026-10-18
action_items: Sofia Chen - Share KMS onboarding doc and list of Redwood subprocessors - 2026-10-13
Ethan Park - Prepare small benchmark showing latency difference for EU pinned Dedicated vs Hosted fallback - 2026-10-15
Maya Patel - Share Nimbus data residency policy and legal must-haves for DPA - 2026-10-12
source.summary: Customer is evaluating Redwood for EU-sensitive analytics workflows. Primary concerns: firm data residency in EU, customer-managed keys, explicit subprocessor list and cross-border fallbacks, audit log retention and access via RBAC. Discussed Redwood Private and Dedicated options, KMS models (hosted vs customer-managed HSM), and contractual controls (DPA + SCCs). Agreed to run an EU-pin throughput/latency benchmark and exchange legal/security artifacts.
source.transcript:
Header:
Date: 2026-10-06
Start time: 14:00 UTC
Duration: 52 minutes
Location: Zoom
Attendees: Sofia Chen, Ethan Park, Noah Martinez (Redwood); Maya Patel, Liam O'Connor, Priya Rao, Diego Alvarez, Samir Gupta (Nimbus Analytics)

Auto summary: High-level security & residency review. Focus on KMS, subprocessor routing, and contractual guarantees for EU-only processing.

[00:00] Sofia Chen: Hey everyone, thanks for making time. This is Sofia from Redwood, I'm the account lead. With me are Ethan and Noah from our solutions team.
[00:06] Maya Patel: Hi, Maya here. Head of Security at Nimbus. We appreciate it. We have a few legal folks listening too.
[00:12] Liam O'Connor: Liam, platform lead, we'll have some infra questions.
[00:15] Ethan Park: Great. Quick agenda — go over residency options (hosted vs dedicated vs private), KMS models, subprocessor list and contractual mitigations. Then Q&A, and next steps. Sound good?
[00:23] Priya Rao: Yes. We'll lean on security and legal aspects; want to avoid surprises.
[00:27] Noah Martinez: Quick note — I'll share a couple of diagrams in chat showing how traffic flows between regions and where keys live.

[00:35] Ethan Park: So to start, Redwood offers three deployment patterns: our Hosted API, Redwood Dedicated which is reserved capacity, and Redwood Private which can be VPC or on-prem. For EU-only workloads you can pin models to EU Dedicated or run Private in your VPC.
[00:50] Diego Alvarez: When you say "pin" what actually happens if capacity in EU is saturated? Do requests go to US? Because regulatoryly we can't allow egress.
[00:58] Sofia Chen: Great question. By default Hosted will route by region affinity and cost. For customers with EU residency requirements we recommend Redwood Dedicated or Private. Dedicated can be pinned so traffic stays within the region and we will not route to another region except under documented fallbacks.
[01:15] Maya Patel: We need strong contractual language — no cross-border processing without consent. Will that be in DPA?
[01:22] Sofia Chen: Yes, our DPA includes region controls and we can add an exhibit that states explicit routing commitments. For emergency fallbacks we have a documented playbook and require customer approval to enable temporary cross-border routing.
[01:35] Samir Gupta: What about sub-processors — we need a current registry and also push notification if there are changes.
[01:42] Ethan Park: We maintain a subprocessor inventory. We'll share the current list after the call. We do provide notification windows and can discuss contractually required notice periods.
[01:51] Priya Rao: Is Redwood a data controller or processor in your standard DPA?
[01:55] Sofia Chen: Processor. We operate as processor for hosted/Dedicated; customers maintain control of data.

[02:05] Liam O'Connor: KMS question: we want customer-managed keys in an EU KMS. Can Redwood support that or do you host keys?
[02:13] Noah Martinez: We support several KMS modes. Option A: Redwood-managed keys with region-specific keystores; Option B: Customer-managed keys via cloud KMS (you provide a key in your GCP/Azure/AWS project in EU region); Option C: HSM / KMS in your VPC for Private deployments.
[02:32] Maya Patel: For compliance we prefer option B or C — keys must be under our tenancy and not exportable.
[02:38] Ethan Park: That's supported. For Dedicated we can accept customer-managed cloud KMS keys — we have a key-wrapping flow and logs for key usage. For Private, you can bring your own HSM and we never have access.
[02:52] Diego Alvarez: How are key operations audited? We need immutable logs with export to our SIEM.
[02:58] Noah Martinez: We log all KMS calls and provide tamper-evident audit trails. Logs can be forwarded to your SIEM via secure forwarding or pulled through our Console API. Retention windows can be configured.

[03:15] Maya Patel: There's also the issue of model caching and prefix caching — could something be cached in a different region inadvertently?
[03:22] Ethan Park: Good call. Our runtime has KV/prefix caching and it's region-local when using region-pinned Dedicated or Private. For Hosted multi-region we have global caches unless you opt out.
[03:32] Samir Gupta: So to be crystal clear — if we pin to an EU Dedicated cluster, neither model weights nor ephemeral tokens will leave the EU?
[03:39] Noah Martinez: Correct, in that configuration model artifacts and KV caches remain in the EU cluster. We will document the boundaries.

[03:50] Priya Rao: On encryption at rest — do you support disk-level encryption with customer-managed keys?
[03:56] Ethan Park: Yes. For Dedicated we can enable CMEK (customer-managed encryption keys) at the block/storage layer. For Private deployments you control the keys entirely.

[04:10] Maya Patel: For cross-border backup scenarios — backups, snapshots, do you replicate across regions?
[04:17] Sofia Chen: By default snapshots are region-scoped for Dedicated/Private. For Hosted, there could be cross-region replication for resilience unless disabled. We can add an exhibit to DPA to prevent cross-region backup replication.
[04:30] Liam O'Connor: Regarding audit logs and RBAC — can we create roles that limit who at Redwood can access telemetry or logs? We want separation of duties.
[04:38] Noah Martinez: Yes, our Console supports RBAC with fine-grained roles. For Dedicated we can further restrict support access and create emergency break glass flows. We can also require SSO via SAML/OIDC and enforce MFA.
[04:51] Samir Gupta: And how would Redwood access our environment for incident response — SSH? Runbooks?
[04:56] Ethan Park: For Dedicated we have a ticketed support flow. Access is audited and short-lived. For Private on-prem we'd only connect with your explicit approval and all sessions are logged.

[05:08] Maya Patel: Legal question — do you offer Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or other data transfer mechanisms?
[05:14] Sofia Chen: Yes, we include SCCs in our DPA for any cross-border transfers. For EU-only contracts we typically avoid transfers. We'll share the legal excerpt.
[05:23] Priya Rao: Do you have SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001 certifications? We need evidence for audits.
[05:28] Ethan Park: We are SOC 2 Type II certified and have ISO 27001. We'll provide the SOC 2 report under NDA and an ISO certificate.

[05:40] Diego Alvarez: One more technical — what happens if a node in EU loses access to the KMS in region; do you fail open or fail closed? We need deterministic behavior.
[05:48] Noah Martinez: By default we fail closed for key operations — requests that require key unwrap will not succeed without KMS access. For availability-sensitive calls we can offer a configurable failover policy with explicit customer approval that may route to a standby key in a secondary EU region, but not to US.
[06:06] Maya Patel: That's helpful. Can you also surface the key usage metrics to our security team?
[06:10] Ethan Park: Yes, key usage and per-request latency are available through the Console and can be exported to your monitoring.

[06:18] Sofia Chen: Wanted to address subprocessor cadence — we provide a 30-day notice for new subprocessors by default, but we can negotiate longer for enterprise contracts.
[06:27] Priya Rao: We prefer 60 days and a right to object for critical subprocessors.
[06:32] Sofia Chen: Noted — we'll flag that for legal.

[06:40] Liam O'Connor: Performance question — what's the latency penalty for customer-managed keys vs Redwood-managed?
[06:45] Ethan Park: There's typically a few tens of milliseconds overhead on first token generation due to key wrap/unwrap, then steady-state per-token costs are negligible. For interactions where we call your KMS per-request, it can be higher; that's why we do local key caches with short TTLs while still maintaining cryptographic protections. We'll run a quick benchmark for your workload.

[07:05] Maya Patel: From an audit perspective we need to see proof of who accessed what. Can you provide a readout that ties a log entry to a user identity?
[07:12] Noah Martinez: Yes, all Console actions map to identities via SSO and are present in the audit log with request IDs for traceability.

[07:20] Priya Rao: On the DPA side — we need contractual wording about no subprocessor change without review for certain categories. Can Redwood include that?
[07:28] Sofia Chen: We can include an exhibit restricting changes to critical subprocessors and a process for review. We'll surface options in the DPA redline.

[07:36] Diego Alvarez: A corner case — what if a model needs a pretrained index that was imported from the US, but inference happens in EU. Is that considered cross-border processing?
[07:45] Ethan Park: If the artifact contains personal data and its origin implies transfer, that could be treated as cross-border. We recommend ingesting or copying artifacts to EU-only storage to avoid transfer. We'll document steps for artifact residency.

[08:00] Maya Patel: Are there any limitations in Redwood Optimize or other product features when running Private/Dedicated? We rely on Smart batching and caching for cost savings.
[08:08] Noah Martinez: Optimize features work in Dedicated and Private, although some centralized analytics may be limited in Private deployments unless you opt into secure telemetry sharing. We can provide local-only metrics as needed.

[08:24] Sofia Chen: To summarize — Redwood can support EU residency via Dedicated or Private, supports customer-managed KMS (cloud KMS or HSM), provides audit logs and RBAC, and we can amend the DPA/SCCs per your needs. Next steps: share subprocessors, run an EU-pin benchmark, exchange DPA legal artifacts, and schedule KMS onboarding demo.

[08:45] Maya Patel: That sounds good. One final ask — can you provide a brief runbook for incident response that shows how Redwood notifies and what information you provide?
[08:53] Ethan Park: Yes, we'll include an incident response runbook and example notifications.

[09:00] Sofia Chen: Great — thanks everyone. We'll follow up with the documents and a proposed time for the benchmark run.
[09:06] Priya Rao: Thanks, bye.
[09:08] Liam O'Connor: Thanks all.
[09:10] End of call.

Notes on transcript quality: Some product names transcribed as "Redwood Dedicaed" or "Redwood Privet" in places — please refer to official docs for exact naming.

source.title: Runtime tuning workshop: sparse routing, ROPE scaling & KV strategies
customer_attendees: Dr. Victor Cheng - Head of ML
topics: MoE routing costs and token affinity
Batching strategies for mixed short/long requests
VPC/on-prem network egress and KMS considerations
source.summary: Extended technical deep-dive with Nimbus to scope runtime optimizations for hybrid Llama/Mistral/Qwen workloads with a Sparse (MoE) experiment path. Key outcomes: agreed to run a mixed-load benchmark, test reduced ROPE delta for long-context drift, prototype KV sharding, and validate quantized variants for throughput gains. Security and VPC constraints noted; next step deliverables and a pilot timeline established.
action_items: Ethan - share benchmark config + perf spreadsheet - due 2026-04-22
Sofia - prepare suggested quantization knobs (4-bit/8-bit config) and expected regressions - due 2026-04-24
Maya - provide current infra topology diagram, VPC peering details, and KMS provider info - due 2026-04-21
Victor - select 3 representative prompt sets (short chat, long context doc, retrieval-augmented) - due 2026-04-20
Liam - confirm procurement timeline for Dedicated trial (30 days) - due 2026-04-28
next_steps: Run 72-hour mixed-workload benchmark using Redwood hosted + Dedicated nodes (Ethan to send config)
Prototype KV-shard layout on Nimbus dev cluster and report hit/miss rates
Quantization smoke test on Mistral-7B and Qwen-7B variants
Security handoff: share SOC2 overview and KMS integration notes
source.transcript:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-04-18
Start: 15:00 UTC
Duration: ~75 minutes
Attendees: Ethan Park (Redwood AE), Sofia Martinez (Redwood SE), Dr. Victor Cheng (Nimbus), Maya Rios (Nimbus), Liam O'Connor (Nimbus CTO)

[00:00:12] Ethan (Redwood AE): Hey everyone, thanks for carving out time. Quick agenda: deep dive on runtime tuning for mixed Llama/Mistral/Qwen fleet, MoE routing cost nitty-gritty, rope scaling experiments, KV cache sharding, and quantization tradeoffs. We wanna align on a pilot.

[00:00:47] Victor (Nimbus): Hey, thanks. High level — our production workload is chatty with lots of short prompts but also periodic long context inference (up to 16k tokens). We run a mix: retrieval-augmented generation for docs, and some few-shot chains for decision automation. Concerned about token costs with MoE routing and also the long-context drift with rope.

[00:01:20] Sofia (Redwood SE): Got it. On MoE — it's a classic token-affinity cost. If routing isn't constrained we see routing blowouts where a few experts get hot. We can configure capacity factors and token routing thresholds, also top-k gating to limit per-token expert count so you don't explode GPU usage.

[00:02:05] Maya (Nimbus): When you say top-k gating — is that per-token or per-batch? We saw in our tests that per-batch gating had better GPU packing but sometimes higher latency tail.

[00:02:30] Sofia: It's per-token decision but runtime can choose to aggregate gating across micro-batches for efficiency. Tradeoff: better throughput, slightly worse p95. For our Dedicated runtime we can tune the micro-batch window.

[00:03:12] Liam (Nimbus): Also curious about rope scaling. We use ROPE positional encodings with Llama variants and noticed hallucination drift in very long sessions. Is there a guided approach to scale rope or apply decay?

[00:03:40] Ethan: Short answer — yes. Two levers: positional re-basing / chunking and rope decay adjustments. If you reduce the effective ROPE delta you can reduce drift, but you may hurt near-term recency. We want to test progressive decay and also a hybrid rebase at retrieval boundaries.

[00:04:25] Victor: Rebase at retrieval boundaries — can you flesh that out?

[00:04:40] Sofia: Basically, when you inject retrieved context you re-anchor positions for the model so the ROPE offsets don't keep growing. Practically we break the long prompt into segments and call the model with stateful KV cache between segments but rebase positions when retrieval happens. There's some cost to re-embedding offsets but upshot is more consistent attention.

[00:05:22] Maya: Does that interact badly with quantized KV? We plan to use 4-bit for weights in production to save cost.

[00:05:40] Sofia: Quantized weights are mostly independent of KV numeric representation, but if you quantize activations or compress KV entries you might see precision loss with long contexts. We recommend 8-bit KV for long-context and 4-bit for the model weights is often OK, but needs evals. We'll ship expected regression numbers.

[00:06:15] Ethan: Note: with Qwen we saw better robustness to 4-bit on retrieval tasks than older Llama-derivatives — but that varies per model and per task. We'll benchmark Llama-2-70B- and Mistral-7B-quant variants as baseline.

[00:07:00] Victor: On KV sharding — we have a multi-AZ setup and want to keep KV local to the GPU rack to avoid cross-AZ latency. How do you handle KV consistency with shard eviction?

[00:07:28] Sofia: Common pattern is local shard with a small global index. We do LRU within local cache, and on miss we fetch from a central store with async warm-up. For Dedicated, we can co-locate a warmed shard per rack. There's a tradeoff: larger local caches reduce misses, but memory pressure increases.

[00:08:10] Maya: Is the central store assumed to be S3-like object storage or Redis? We worry about write amplification.

[00:08:28] Sofia: Both are supported. Redis/KeyDB works for low-latency hot fetches; object store is cheaper for cold stores. We also have a hybrid TTL/LSM approach — small hot tier in Redis, cold tier in object storage. We'll provide a recommended config for your workload.

[00:09:05] Liam: Let's talk about batching. Our workload distribution is heavy on 1-4 token prompts and occasional 12k token documents. How do we avoid batching hurting p50 for chats but still get throughput for docs?

[00:09:30] Ethan: Adaptive batching is the answer — dynamic window size based on request length distribution, with weighted priorities so short requests get prioritized. Redwood runtime uses size-aware batching to avoid packing long sequences that delay short ones. You can set strict p95 SLO pins to preempt long batches.

[00:10:02] Victor: Is that user-configurable per-route? We have one API for chat and another for embeddings; want distinct policies.

[00:10:18] Sofia: Yes, per-route config. You can pin a latency SLO and cost target; we will pick batching/window settings that try to match. Also you can route heavy long-context jobs to Dedicated where throughput is higher and extra GPUs help.

[00:11:00] Maya: VPC considerations — we need a private deployment or VPC peering. KMS is through our provider (HashiCorp Vault and AWS KMS). How does Redwood Private integrate?

[00:11:25] Ethan: Redwood Private supports bring-your-own-KMS and HSM integrations; for VPC we do a private control-plane with customer-managed peering, and the inference workers can live in your subnet. Audit logs are forwarded to your SIEM. We will provide a deployment checklist.

[00:11:58] Victor: Any compliance gotchas with quantization? Does encryption-at-rest still work for model weights if we use local NVMe caches?

[00:12:18] Sofia: Yes — encryption at rest is orthogonal. If you cache quantized weights on NVMe, you still encrypt the disk; keys are in KMS. Evaluate cold start times since decrypt + load + dequantize is added overhead. We'll measure that in the benchmark.

[00:12:52] Ethan: Back to MoE costs — two practical knobs: capacity_factor and expert_dropout/gating entropy regularization during training (if you control model), but at serving time you can cap experts per token or route to a distilled dense fallback when certain capacity thresholds trigger.

[00:13:28] Victor: Distilled dense fallback sounds attractive for cost predictability. How big is the quality drop?

[00:13:45] Sofia: It varies — for classification-like tasks small; for creative generation more noticeable. We recommend an A/B rollout on a small percentage of traffic first and compare n-gram overlap and human evals. We'll help with rollout tooling.

[00:14:20] Maya: Practical ask — can you provide example configs for capacity_factor and gating thresholds that reduced our costs in comparable customers?

[00:14:36] Ethan: Yes. Action item: we'll send a config spreadsheet with recommended start points for each model family (Llama, Mistral, Qwen) and for 7B/13B/70B classes.

[00:15:10] Victor: Regarding rope scaling: you mentioned progressive decay. How do we implement without retraining?

[00:15:30] Sofia: You can apply positional scaling in the runtime layer by multiplying attention-phase contributions by a decay factor that increases with token distance. No retrain needed — it's a runtime shim. Caveat: it can subtly shift token importance; test on QA accuracy.

[00:16:05] Liam: Are there standard decay functions you recommend? Linear, exponential?

[00:16:18] Sofia: We generally try a gentle exponential where weight ~ exp(-alpha * distance) with alpha tuned to your average document length. Also step-wise rebase works well when retrieval occurs. We'll include scripts in the repo.

[00:16:50] Ethan: Quick interjection — operationally we want to keep the p99 under control. For that we instrument gating metrics and expert skew. If we detect >X% of tokens routing to top-1 expert, we can trigger a soft fallback policy.

[00:17:25] Victor: Can that auto-trigger a model switch? We have a lightweight dense model that could serve as fallback.

[00:17:37] Sofia: Yes, runtime can do model-level fallbacks by route policy. You can set conditions (latency or routing hotness) to route to dense. It's part of our smart routing feature.

[00:18:05] Maya: Networking nuance — if fallback routes to Dedicated nodes in a different region, do we see cross-region latency? How to avoid that?

[00:18:22] Ethan: We'll keep fallbacks within the same region or AZ by policy. If not possible, we prefer warm local smaller dense replicas to avoid cross-region hops.

[00:18:50] Victor: Let's talk concrete benchmark plan. We want: 1) short-chat p50/p95/p99, 2) long-doc throughput/latency, 3) MoE cost per 1M tokens with and without gating caps.

[00:19:12] Sofia: Agreed. We'll run three workloads: chat (1-8 tokens), multi-turn doc sessions (avg 6k tokens), and a mixed 80/15/5 short/medium/long split. We'll instrument token-level routing stats, KV hit rates, cold start times for quantized weights.

[00:19:45] Liam: How long to get initial numbers?

[00:19:58] Ethan: We can stand up a 72-hour run on hosted + Dedicated mix; initial results within 4 business days after configs are agreed.

[00:20:20] Victor: Good. A few more nitty-gritty things — memory planning for KV shards: what ratio of KV memory to model memory do you recommend?

[00:20:38] Sofia: Rule of thumb: for 8k contexts, KV memory can be ~10-20% of model activation memory, but it scales with number of parallel inferences. We'll give you a table per model size.

[00:21:05] Maya: Do you support heterogeneous racks where some GPUs are 80GB and others 48GB? We want to leverage both.

[00:21:22] Sofia: Yes — the scheduler can pack models to GPUs with capacity awareness and we can pin large-models to 80GB nodes while smaller workloads land on 48GB; the runtime abstracts that. There's a small additional cost for model placement and shuffling for cache warm-ups.

[00:21:50] Victor: Any gotchas for Mistral vs Llama vs Qwen when doing quantization? We've seen Mistral be fragile at 4-bit in our early runs.

[00:22:10] Sofia: Mistral architectures sometimes require non-uniform quantization or per-channel scales to maintain quality. We'll provide quantization recipes: per-channel symmetric for Mistral, standard GPTQ-like for Llama, and a tuned 4-bit recipe for Qwen. Expect to run small evals to pick the best.

[00:22:45] Ethan: Also mention that activations quantization is more dangerous than weight-only. We advise weight-only first.

[00:23:10] Victor: Security question — audit logs. We need request-level logs but with PII redaction. Can Redwood redact before export?

[00:23:28] Ethan: Yes, we offer token-level redaction hooks and PII masking pipelines before logs leave the private environment. We can also limit retention windows per your policy.

[00:24:00] Maya: For the pilot, any recommended default SLO numbers to start with?

[00:24:15] Sofia: For chat routes start with p50 < 120ms, p95 < 350ms for small models on Dedicated; for long-doc throughput aim for X tokens/s depending on model size — we'll give concrete numbers in the spreadsheet.

[00:24:45] Victor: Could you share an example of routing config that gates to 2 experts max and falls back to dense if top-1 skew > 60%?

[00:25:02] Ethan: We'll include a sample YAML with those thresholds and traffic-splitting for canary.

[00:25:30] Liam: One operational question — model loading times for quantized vs non-quantized. How much time added for dequant?

[00:25:48] Sofia: Loading quantized weights from NVMe and dequantizing adds ~3-12 seconds depending on model size and NVMe speed. We reduce that with persistent warm nodes or lazy load strategies.

[00:26:20] Victor: Anything else on cost math? We want a per-token cost estimate that accounts for MoE expert-hours and KV-warmth.

[00:26:37] Ethan: We'll produce a cost model: base per-token compute + expert surcharge + KV miss penalty + storage egress + amortized model RAM. You'll get a spreadsheet to play with variables.

[00:27:05] Maya: On the KV miss penalty — what's typical overhead to fetch from cold store (S3) vs warmed Redis?

[00:27:20] Sofia: Redis hit ~1-2ms, cold fetch S3 can be 50-150ms depending on object size and transfer. That's why the hybrid hot/cold is important.

[00:27:50] Victor: Ok great. Let's open to Q&A and real examples we can try after the call.

[00:28:10] Liam: Quick one — do you support deterministic routing for MoE (e.g., hashed token routing) to improve cache locality?

[00:28:25] Sofia: Deterministic hash routing can help locality but can produce hot experts. We prefer popularity-based learned gating plus soft caps, but hashed routing is an option for predictable locality. We'll prototype both.

[00:29:00] Ethan: We'll stop here and summarize.

[00:29:12] Ethan: Recap: 72-hour benchmark agreed, Redwood to send configs, Nimbus to provide prompt sets and infra diagram. Action items captured. We'll run quantization smoke tests and KV shard prototype. Security and VPC checklist to be shared.

[00:29:45] Victor: Sounds good. Thanks all.

[00:30:00] Ethan: Thanks — we'll follow up with the spreadsheet and sample runtime YAMLs.

End of meeting transcript (notes: transcription imperfect — some acronyms mis-transcribed, e.g., ROPE sometimes shown as "rope" or "R.O.P.E", KV as "K V" in raw output).

title: Runtime tuning workshop: sparse routing, ROPE scaling & KV strategies
preview: Extended technical deep-dive with Nimbus to scope runtime optimizations for hybrid Llama/Mistral/Qwen workloads with a Sparse (MoE) experiment path. Key outcomes: agreed to run a mixed-load benchmark, test reduced ROPE delta for long-context drift, prototype KV sharding, and validate quantized variants for throughput gains. Security and VPC constraints noted; next step deliverables and a pilot timeline established.
